_README: Agent-produced facets (Step B). Keyed by items.paper_full_text_path. Each facet is {v, note}: v = canonical value (recurs to connect papers), note = how THIS paper relates to it. _definitions[dimension::value] = what the tag means. Re-run apply-facets.mjs after editing (idempotent). PROGRESS: HCI & Adaptive Interfaces 21/21 faceted; Emotional Design 68/68 faceted (10 non-papers/dupes skipped). REMAINING: AI Personalization & SLR (226). Vocabulary = 63 canonical values across the 5 dimensions; reuse it before coining new values.

_definitions.ai_technique::Human-AI interaction: Designing and studying how people interact with AI systems as collaborators or tools.
_definitions.ai_technique::Adaptive/intelligent UI: Interfaces (AUI/IUI) that adjust their layout, content, or behaviour to the user, often using AI.
_definitions.ai_technique::User modeling: Building a representation of a user's preferences, abilities, or context to drive personalization.
_definitions.ai_technique::Machine learning: Algorithms that learn patterns from data to power prediction or adaptation.
_definitions.ai_technique::Autonomous agents & automa: Systems that act on the user's behalf with varying degrees of independence.
_definitions.ai_technique::Human-AI co-creation: People and generative AI producing content or designs together, sharing agency.
_definitions.ai_technique::Generative AI: AI that produces new content (text, images, designs) as an active creative collaborator.
_definitions.ux_effect::Participation & collaboration: Designing with and among users and stakeholders rather than only for them.
_definitions.ux_effect::User engagement: How strongly an experience draws users in and sustains their attention and interest.
_definitions.ux_effect::Wayfinding & spatial orientat: How people locate themselves and navigate within physical or virtual space.
_definitions.ux_effect::Sense of place: The felt, qualitative character and meaning a person attaches to a space.
_definitions.ux_effect::Personalization & adaptation: Tailoring the experience to an individual user's needs, context, or behaviour.
_definitions.ux_effect::Usability: How easily and effectively users can accomplish their tasks with a system.
_definitions.ux_effect::Immersion & presence: The degree to which a (often virtual) environment makes the user feel 'really there'.
_definitions.ux_effect::Emotional response: The feelings a design or place evokes in the user.
_definitions.ux_effect::Experiential learning: Learning through direct, often immersive, first-hand experience.
_definitions.ux_effect::Behaviour change: Designing technology to shift users' habits or actions over time.
_definitions.ux_effect::Trust: A user's willingness to rely on a system under uncertainty.
_definitions.challenge::Stakeholder collaboration: Coordinating the differing needs of users, communities, and decision-makers.
_definitions.challenge::Adapting to diverse users: Serving users who differ in ability, culture, context, or expertise.
_definitions.challenge::Measuring user experience: Capturing subjective, multi-faceted experience in valid, comparable ways.
_definitions.challenge::Disorientation: Users losing their sense of position or direction in a space or interface.
_definitions.challenge::Communicating intangible/sens: Conveying the felt, sensory, or qualitative aspects of a place or experience.
_definitions.challenge::Fragmented research: A scattered, under-consolidated body of work that is hard to synthesize.
_definitions.challenge::AI unpredictability: AI systems making unexpected or hard-to-anticipate errors.
_definitions.challenge::Designing for uncertainty: Designing when a system's capabilities or outputs are not fully predictable.
_definitions.challenge::Motion sickness: Discomfort (nausea, disorientation) induced by immersive VR/AR.
_definitions.metric::Qualitative interviews: Insights gathered from semi-structured interviews or open feedback.
_definitions.metric::Survey responses: Data collected from questionnaires across many respondents.
_definitions.metric::Wayfinding performance: Objective measures of navigation success (time, errors, route).
_definitions.metric::Behavioural measures: Observed actions and behaviour used as evidence, rather than self-report.
_definitions.metric::Usability evaluation: Assessing ease-of-use via tasks, heuristics, or usability testing.
_definitions.metric::Presence rating: Self-reported sense of 'being there' in a virtual environment.
_definitions.metric::Ownership & realism: Felt body-ownership and perceived realism of a virtual experience.
_definitions.metric::Engagement metrics: Indicators of enjoyment, attention, or sustained involvement.
_definitions.metric::Self-report measures: Data from users reporting their own attitudes or states (e.g. questionnaires).
_definitions.metric::Physiological measures: Bodily signals (e.g. heart rate, gaze) used as objective indicators.
_definitions.ethical_concern::User empowerment: Giving users genuine voice, control, and benefit in the system.
_definitions.ethical_concern::User autonomy & agency: Preserving the user's capacity to decide and act for themselves.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Digital divide: Unequal access to technology across people and groups.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Societal impact of AI: Broader, sometimes harmful, effects of AI on society.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Inclusion & accessibili: Ensuring a system works for the widest possible range of people.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Behaviour manipulation: The risk of nudging or persuading users in ways that may not serve them.
_definitions.ai_technique::Affective computing: Computing that senses, models, expresses, or responds to human emotion (Picard's term).
_definitions.ai_technique::Emotion recognition: Automatically inferring a person's emotional state from face, voice, text, or physiology.
_definitions.ai_technique::Deep learning: Multi-layer neural networks (e.g. CNNs, LSTMs) that learn representations directly from data.
_definitions.ux_effect::Aesthetic pleasure: Enjoyment arising from the beauty and sensory appeal of a product or interface.
_definitions.ux_effect::Satisfaction: Contentment from a product meeting or exceeding the user's needs and expectations.
_definitions.ux_effect::Persuasion: Shifting a user's attitudes, motivation, or intentions through design.
_definitions.ux_effect::Flow: Total, energized absorption in an activity where challenge meets skill.
_definitions.ux_effect::Well-being: Designing technology to support psychological health and positive functioning.
_definitions.ux_effect::Emotional attachment: A lasting affective bond a person forms with a product, place, or memory.
_definitions.challenge::Measuring emotion: Capturing subjective, fleeting emotional states in valid, comparable ways.
_definitions.challenge::Designing for emotion: Deliberately shaping a design to evoke specific intended emotions.
_definitions.challenge::Defining emotion: The lack of scientific consensus on what an emotion fundamentally is.
_definitions.challenge::Bridging theory and practice: Turning emotion and affect theory into methods designers can actually apply.
_definitions.metric::Emotion scales (SAM/PAD): Standardized rating instruments for emotion (e.g. SAM, PAD, PrEmo, PANAS, DEQ).
_definitions.metric::Facial expression analysis: Inferring emotion from coded or automatically detected facial expressions.
_definitions.metric::Eye tracking: Gaze, fixation, and pupil measures used to index attention and affect.
_definitions.metric::EEG / neural measures: Brain-activity signals (e.g. EEG) used to study emotional and cognitive states.
_definitions.metric::Controlled experiment: Comparing manipulated conditions to test an emotional or design effect.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Emotional manipulation: Exploiting users' emotions to influence them in ways that may not serve them.
_definitions.ethical_concern::Privacy: Protecting intimate data, especially the emotional and physiological signals affective systems capture.

uxEffects.note: The seminal UTOPIA project — workers, unions, and researchers co-designing workplace IT.
Sustained, active involvement of workplace users throughout development.

challenges.note: Aligns workers, trade unions, and researchers in the Scandinavian tradition.
Confronts broadening use contexts beyond the original workplace.

ethicalConcerns.note: Centers worker voice and control over the technology shaping their work.

uxEffects.note: A museum web app designed explicitly to grow audience engagement.
Human-centred design framework aimed at an easy, useful museum site.
Stakeholder interviews and personas shape a co-designed plan.

challenges.note: Gauges the experience through observation sessions and testing.

metrics.note: Five stakeholder interviews and four observation sessions.

uxEffects.note: How residents vs. strangers orient using reference points.
Reference points give a city legibility and meaning.

challenges.note: Poor visual readability leaves people unable to orient.

uxEffects.note: 'Interactive architecture' adapts the design to all factors and parameters.
A multi-relational process engaging many aspects of design.

challenges.note: Seeks optimum adaption across many competing design factors.

uxEffects.note: Evaluates wayfinding behaviour in complex buildings.
A user-centred approach to building usability.

challenges.note: Tackles the feeling of being 'lost' in unfamiliar buildings.
Empirically evaluates UX alongside wayfinding behaviour.

metrics.note: Measures navigation behaviour in architectural environments.
Observes how people actually move and find their way.

uxEffects.note: Surveys VR/AR systems that immerse designers in the design.
Proposes CORAULIS for hands-on, pedagogical design.
Supports collaborative design among designers, laymen, and decision-makers.

challenges.note: Weighs device advantages and limits for varied users.

uxEffects.note: Participatory urban planning via community emotional mapping.
Maps citizens' emotions about urban space.
Captures how places feel — attractiveness, safety.

challenges.note: Feeds 2,117 residents' input into the city's strategic plan.

metrics.note: 2,117 respondents marked 25,760 points and 4,801 comments.

uxEffects.note: Uses VR to visualize urban transformation.
Brings citizen input and feedback to the forefront.
Debates future visions of the livable city.

challenges.note: Visualizing urbanism's transformation for stakeholders.

ethicalConcerns.note: Asserts citizen voice in shaping the city.

uxEffects.note: An immersive VR prototype for experiencing urban places.
Foregrounds the sensory, intangible aspects of place.
Multi-sensory soundscapes evoke the feel of a place.

challenges.note: Conveys sensory/qualitative place aspects that resist text or graphs.
Seeks valid evaluation of the immersive approach.

metrics.note: Feedback gathered from participants and observers.

uxEffects.note: VR places students inside urban-design scenarios.
VR as an active, participative educational methodology.
Architecture students and professionals take part.

challenges.note: Fits tech-savvy learners across varied subjects.

uxEffects.note: A series for sharing ideas across design fields.

challenges.note: Attempts to map a varied, scattered design-research landscape.

uxEffects.note: Cyberspace as a new, transposed virtual experience of the city.
The 'digital city' reframes the lived experience of space.

challenges.note: Digitalizes experience beyond physical and sensory interaction.

ethicalConcerns.note: Notes its use is not available to everyone.
Raises the ethics of mediated, digitalized experience.

aiTechniques.note: Examines why human-AI interaction is uniquely hard to design.

uxEffects.note: AI's adaptive, complex output shapes the user experience.

challenges.note: AI makes unpredictable errors that damage UX.
Uncertainty around AI's capabilities and output complexity.

ethicalConcerns.note: AI errors can lead to undesired societal impact.

aiTechniques.note: Lays out the foundations of adaptive/intelligent UIs (AUI/IUI).
Building an accurate user preference model is key.
ML training and convergence underpin adaptive interfaces.

uxEffects.note: Reviews universal usability across 165 papers.
Plasticity — interfaces that adapt to users and contexts.

challenges.note: Interfaces must serve users of varied abilities and cultures.
Synthesizes 55 years of scattered work into a taxonomy.

metrics.note: Assesses the plasticity and usability of adaptive UIs.

ethicalConcerns.note: 'Universal usability' — interfaces that work for everyone.

uxEffects.note: AR overlays immersive interaction onto the city.
Human-city interaction within connected smart cities.

challenges.note: Reviews 260 articles and flags under-explored issues.
Constrained interfaces and input across many contexts.

uxEffects.note: Behavioural Intervention Technologies (BITs) to shift user behaviour.
Human-centred BITs tailored to behaviour-change processes.

challenges.note: Consolidates 28 scattered publications into a synthesis.

ethicalConcerns.note: Designing technology to deliberately change behaviour.
Raises who controls the intended behaviour change.

uxEffects.note: Full-immersion VR for situated experiential education.
VR stands in for declining residential fieldtrips.

challenges.note: Motion sickness emerged as a key negative experience.
Applies a three-facet UX analytical frame.

metrics.note: Semi-structured interviews with staff and students.
Assesses the felt sense of the VR experience.

aiTechniques.note: Concerns trust in automated and autonomous agents.

uxEffects.note: A comprehensive review of Trust in Automation (TiA).

challenges.note: How to validly capture the complex trust construct.

metrics.note: Catalogs self-report measures of trust.
Catalogs behavioural indices of trust.
Catalogs physiological indices of trust.

uxEffects.note: Hand-tracking and grasping visualizations for immersive VR.

challenges.note: Tests whether lab effects hold in a real game.

metrics.note: Measures presence in a VR escape-room game.
Measures body-ownership and perceived realism.
Measures enjoyment and efficiency.

uxEffects.note: A UX-driven maker office space designed to engage users.
Identifies design points for a usable office space.
Atmosphere creation for the office experience.

challenges.note: Analyzes user demand and experience to guide design.

aiTechniques.note: A scoping review of human-AI co-creation through the lens of agency.
Treats AI as an active creative collaborator.

uxEffects.note: Maps control mechanisms: input, action, output, feedback.
Studies how agency is shared in co-creation.

challenges.note: Configurations of agency and control are still unsettled.
Synthesizes 134 papers over 20 years into one framework.

ethicalConcerns.note: Centers the distribution and dynamic of human agency.

uxEffects.note: Maps the structure of human emotional experience and expression.

challenges.note: Argues the 'basic six' is too coarse; proposes a high-dimensional emotion space.
Derives emotion dimensions from large-scale ratings of experience and expression.

metrics.note: Builds the taxonomy from people's self-reported emotional experiences.

uxEffects.note: Shows distinct emotions arise from patterns along cognitive-appraisal dimensions.

challenges.note: Refines emotion theory via appraisal dimensions that distinguish emotions.

metrics.note: Participants rated recalled emotional experiences on appraisal scales.

uxEffects.note: Examines the nature and components of emotional experience.

challenges.note: Surveys the long philosophical struggle over what an emotion really is.

uxEffects.note: Critiques the idea of a small fixed set of biologically basic emotions.

challenges.note: Questions the widespread assumption that discrete 'basic emotions' exist.

uxEffects.note: Kansei design integrates aesthetic and emotional factors into product form.
Designs products around users' subjective, affective ('Kansei') responses.

challenges.note: Balances technical demands with users' aesthetic and experiential needs.

uxEffects.note: Argues product pleasure must go beyond usability as a differentiator.
Positions pleasure as the next step once usability is mastered.
Pleasurable products are used more and shape future purchase choices.

metrics.note: Surveys behavioural correlates of pleasure in product use.

uxEffects.note: Outlines how emotions, distinct from mood and affect, drive consumer responses.
Examines emotions' role in marketing and goal-directed consumer behaviour.

challenges.note: Reviews measurement issues for emotions in consumer research.
Differentiates emotion from affect, moods, and attitudes via appraisal theory.

aiTechniques.note: Surveys how systems can recognize and respond to user emotion.

uxEffects.note: Foundational account of emotion's role across human–computer interaction.

challenges.note: Reviews distinguishing emotion from mood and capturing it in HCI.

metrics.note: Covers self-report alongside autonomic and facial measures of emotion.
Discusses facial expression as a channel for reading emotion.

uxEffects.note: Analyses how products elicit emotions from disgust to desire.
Links product appearance and meaning to evoked positive emotions.

challenges.note: Frames how designers can intentionally elicit product emotions.

aiTechniques.note: Defends affective computing and maps its open research challenges.
Concerns machines that recognize, model, and respond to emotion.

challenges.note: Addresses criticisms about reliably sensing emotional information.

ethicalConcerns.note: Raises concerns about machines capturing intimate emotional information.

uxEffects.note: Concerns the body of research on designing for emotion.

challenges.note: Asks why design-and-emotion research has not improved design practice.
Critiques the scattered, low-impact state of design-and-emotion theory.

uxEffects.note: Reviews Norman's case that emotion is central to design.
Discusses Norman's shift from usability to attractive, pleasurable things.

challenges.note: Engages Norman's visceral/behavioural/reflective design framework.

uxEffects.note: Customizable UI 'skins' matched to user personality and preference.
Studies aesthetic skin preferences beyond user-system performance.

challenges.note: Explores how personality differences shape interface-skin choices.

metrics.note: Two explorative studies relate personality to skin preference.

uxEffects.note: Shows specific emotions systematically shape consumer decisions.
Appraisal Tendency Framework predicts emotion effects on choice.

challenges.note: Distinguishes specific emotions by their appraisal tendencies.

uxEffects.note: A 'design for wow' approach targeting pleasant-surprise emotions.
Defines a product character with high 'wow-impact'.

challenges.note: Uses the eliciting conditions of emotions to drive product character.
Proposes a measurable 'wow-index' for the experience.

metrics.note: Combines qualitative and quantitative methods to gauge wow.

uxEffects.note: UX research targets affective qualities, meaning, and value, not just tasks.
Frames UX as extending beyond conventional usability models.

challenges.note: No consensus on defining or validating core UX constructs.
Many competing UX frameworks and incoherent views complicate the field.

uxEffects.note: Treats 'supra-functional' product emotions as competitive differentiators.
Pleasurable emotions beyond function shape product success.

challenges.note: Argues emotion must be designed in once function and usability saturate.

ethicalConcerns.note: Frames emotions as something to 'exploit' for commercial product success.

uxEffects.note: A framework of nine distinct sources of product-evoked emotions.
Maps eliciting conditions for pleasurable product affect.

challenges.note: Gives designers eliciting conditions to target specific product emotions.
Synthesizes three theories of product affect into one structure.

uxEffects.note: A general framework spanning all affective responses to products.
Distinguishes aesthetic, meaning, and emotional levels of experience.

challenges.note: Organizes product experience to guide designing for affect.

uxEffects.note: Surveys emotion's role across human factors and ergonomics design.
Reviews Norman's emotional design and Jordan's four pleasures.
Covers flow and hedonomics as positive-experience goals in HFE.

challenges.note: Maps how HFE shifted from preventing harm to designing positive emotion.
Consolidates scattered emotion-and-design work in human factors.

uxEffects.note: Personas give designers a vivid representation of the target user.
Personas communicate customer needs across the design team.

challenges.note: Uses personas to represent varied user needs in product design.

metrics.note: Delphi method gathers expert consensus on persona benefits.

uxEffects.note: The Behaviour Change Wheel: a method for designing behaviour-change interventions.

challenges.note: Links a behavioural analysis to concrete intervention functions and policies.
Synthesizes 19 existing frameworks into one coherent method.

ethicalConcerns.note: Concerns the deliberate engineering of changes in people's behaviour.
Raises who decides which behaviours ought to change.

uxEffects.note: Applies psychological flow to serious-game design.
Uses positive emotions to deeply immerse users in training games.
Draws on positive psychology to support players' positive functioning.
Serious games as immersive training applications.

challenges.note: Translates positive-psychology constructs into game design.

uxEffects.note: Argues emotions are real as part of socially constructed reality.

challenges.note: Theorizes emotions via socially shared conceptual knowledge, not fixed circuits.

uxEffects.note: Examines unconscious, visceral-level emotional responses to products.
Links visceral reactions to product-design innovation and human values.

challenges.note: Targets the visceral level of Norman's emotional-design model.
Notes limited literature on unconscious behaviour in product design.

uxEffects.note: Treats emotions as cognitive processes with communicative and action-readiness functions.

challenges.note: A cognitive-science account of what emotions are and how they arise.

uxEffects.note: Uses a simulated environment to recreate experiences reported in space travel.
Investigates the felt experiences people report during space travel.

metrics.note: Draws on first-hand reported experiences of space travel.

aiTechniques.note: Proposes research scenarios for emotion recognition across four domains.
Applies emotion-recognition methods to software, web, education, and gaming.

uxEffects.note: Uses recognized emotion to customize websites and adapt experiences.

challenges.note: Assesses feasibility of detecting defined emotional states per domain.

uxEffects.note: Explores the value of unconscious human behaviour in product design.

challenges.note: A new design-thinking perimeter built on unconscious behaviour.

metrics.note: Uses Verbal Protocol Analysis of designers' conceptual sketching.

uxEffects.note: A 'Positive Technology' app to support stress management and well-being.
Guides users through stress-management exercises on mobile.

challenges.note: Applies the Positive Technology paradigm to regulate stress and arousal.

metrics.note: Tracks arousal via heart rate and wearable biosensors.

aiTechniques.note: An emotion-monitoring stand tracking emotion in human–computer interaction.
Verifies procedures for recognizing emotion from physiological signals.

challenges.note: Tackles motion artifacts and sensor placement disturbing physiological readings.

metrics.note: Verifies measurement procedures for physiological emotion indicators.

uxEffects.note: Argues technology's value-ladenness evokes positive and negative emotions.

challenges.note: Positions emotions as gateways to designing for human values.

uxEffects.note: Shows emotions are potent, predictable drivers of decisions.
Maps how incidental and integral emotions influence judgment and choice.

challenges.note: Uses the appraisal-tendency framework to specify emotion effects.

uxEffects.note: Tests whether emotionally designed multimedia improves learning outcomes.
Designs learning environments to evoke positive, neutral, or negative emotions.

challenges.note: Manipulates the emotional design of a multimedia learning environment.

metrics.note: Compares positive/neutral/negative designs on achievement and motivation.

aiTechniques.note: Compares off-the-shelf emotion-recognition solutions.

challenges.note: Assesses accuracy of recognizing emotion from facial expressions.

metrics.note: Benchmarks recognition accuracy from facial expressions.

uxEffects.note: VR as an immersive, ecological tool to induce awe.

challenges.note: Seeks to elicit particularly intense awe experiences.

metrics.note: Uses presence as a key asset of the VR awe-induction approach.

uxEffects.note: A new instrument to measure distinct, state self-reported emotions.

challenges.note: Addresses the lack of validated tools for several discrete emotions.

metrics.note: Introduces and validates the Discrete Emotions Questionnaire (DEQ).
A self-report scale for state emotional experience.

uxEffects.note: Seeks lasting attachment to counter disposable consumption.
Applies Norman's visceral/behavioural/reflective levels to products.

challenges.note: Uses emotional design to address products' ecological impact.

uxEffects.note: Designs technology to promote psychological well-being.
Shapes technology-mediated experiences toward positive affect.

challenges.note: Sets out the Positive Technology agenda for computing and design.

uxEffects.note: Reviews the role of emotional factors in web-interface UX.
Treats emotion as central to web user experience and performance.

challenges.note: Surveys how emotional design is applied to web interfaces.

aiTechniques.note: Reviews emotion-detection technologies and their applications.
Surveys emotion detection as core to affective-computing projects.

challenges.note: Maps the most important aspects of detecting emotion.
Consolidates emotion-detection technologies across a new discipline.

uxEffects.note: A research-through-design approach to add emotional value to products.
Targets richer emotional responses to products like mobile phones.

challenges.note: Builds a theoretical framework plus a non-verbal emotion instrument.

metrics.note: Uses a non-verbal instrument to measure emotional responses.

uxEffects.note: Frames emotions as cognitive processes shaping interaction design.
Builds on Norman's and Jordan's positive-emotion approaches.

challenges.note: Proposes 'applied emotional design' for interactive technologies.
Treats emotions via appraisal and complex-emotion theory.

uxEffects.note: Conceptualizes consumers' emotional engagement with everyday products.
Examines consumer–product interactions within the emotional-design process.

challenges.note: An intensive literature review synthesizing scattered work.
Frames emotional engagement inside the emotional-design process.

aiTechniques.note: Designs affective, agent-based user interfaces.

uxEffects.note: Analyses emotion and motivation to design affective UIs.
Plans UI features for persuasive-technology applications.

challenges.note: A scenario-based method to plan emotion-aware UI features.

uxEffects.note: Centers user emotion in UX-based system development.
Treats the user as the center of the development process.

challenges.note: Compares two measurement methods for UX.
Builds emotional design into a development system.

uxEffects.note: Crowdsources citizens' perceptions of their urban environment.
Maps citizens' emotions about places in the city.
Captures how urban environments are felt by residents.

challenges.note: Brings public participation into the urban-planning process.

ethicalConcerns.note: Gives citizens voice in shaping their environment after decades without it.

aiTechniques.note: Considers giving machines emotions in the 'algorithmic society'.

uxEffects.note: Explores emotion as central to an AI-driven information society.

ethicalConcerns.note: Examines deep societal changes from AI and emotional machines.

challenges.note: Develops a method to generate products around specific emotions.

uxEffects.note: Uses emotional design to forge happy memories and bonds with things.

challenges.note: Designs products to trigger and preserve happy memories.

uxEffects.note: Tests emotional design's effect on learners' performance in multimedia learning.

uxEffects.note: Studies emotions, impressions, and stimulation in digital-library use.

metrics.note: Includes an eye-tracking study of one digital library.

aiTechniques.note: Surveys sensing, modelling, and adapting to emotion in HCI.

uxEffects.note: Reviews two decades of emotion in HCI and its possible futures.

challenges.note: Spans sensing, expressing, regulating, and manipulating emotion.

ethicalConcerns.note: Flags manipulating and regulating emotion as a future concern.

aiTechniques.note: Models emotional state to personalize persuasive systems.

uxEffects.note: Designs personalized, emotion-adaptive persuasive systems.
Persuasive strategies to motivate behaviour change.

ethicalConcerns.note: Adapting persuasion to emotion raises manipulation concerns.

aiTechniques.note: Classifies emotional states for affective computing and HCI.
Classifies emotion from EEG-derived spectrogram images.

challenges.note: Identifies emotional state for mental-health and UX applications.

metrics.note: Derives spectrograms from EEG signals to classify emotion.

uxEffects.note: Examines emotional design in the digital user experience.
Links emotional design to successful digital product design.

challenges.note: Applies emotional design as digital products become a daily norm.

uxEffects.note: Reviews eye behaviour as a window onto emotional and cognitive state.

challenges.note: Surveys eye-tracking metrics for detecting emotion and cognition.
Consolidates eye-tracking metrics for emotional and cognitive processes.

metrics.note: Catalogs gaze, fixation, and pupil metrics of emotion and workload.

aiTechniques.note: Computers beginning to express, recognize, and 'have' emotions.

uxEffects.note: Argues emotion is essential to human cognition and perception.

challenges.note: Examines the role and nature of emotion for affective computers.

uxEffects.note: A prototype approach to awe as a distinct emotion.

challenges.note: Defines awe via perceived vastness and a need for accommodation.
